title: The Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 22, 2019 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, falling within the long stretch of green liturgical calendar between Pentecost and Advent. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), the readings would have been Proverbs 31:10-31 (or Jeremiah 11:18-20), Psalm 54, James 3:16-4:6, and Mark 9:30-37, with the Gospel focusing on Jesus's teaching about humility and welcoming children. The Episcopal Church follows the same RCL cycle as most Protestant denominations, making this a widely-preached set of texts across Christian traditions that Sunday.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of its 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas (June 27-July 4, 2018), so no major convention news was immediate, though implementation of 2018 resolutions continued. The Diocese of Colorado under Bishop Robert Wright was engaged in standard diocesan operations; no major episcopal elections or significant controversies dominated Anglican/Episcopal news in mid-to-late September 2019. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the seat of the bishop, would have been in its typical role as the liturgical and administrative center of the diocese.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical late summer weather transitioning to fall in late September, with mild temperatures and low precipitation risk. Mayor Michael Hancock's administration was focused on affordable housing and homelessness initiatives, issues that had become increasingly visible in the Capitol Hill area where the Cathedral is located. No major weather disasters or extraordinary local crises dominated the week before September 22, making this a relatively stable period for Denver civic life.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump-Ukraine scandal was beginning to break publicly; the whistleblower complaint was filed on August 12 and started reaching media outlets and Congress in the week of September 23-24, making the lead-up week one of political tension and gathering storm. House Democrats were increasingly discussing impeachment inquiries, though formal proceedings had not yet been announced. The 2020 presidential campaign was in full swing with Democratic primary debates occurring (the third Democratic debate had been September 12-13), and climate change was receiving heightened attention after the Global Climate Strike on September 20, 2019.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Hong Kong was in its fourth month of pro-democracy protests, with escalating clashes between demonstrators and police that drew international media attention and humanitarian concerns. The Kashmir region remained tense following India's revocation of Article 370 in August, with ongoing communication restrictions and military presence. The Amazon rainforest fires that had dominated August news cycles were still actively burning and under discussion as a global environmental crisis.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The broader American mood was one of political polarization and anxiety about democracy, with Trump's presidency remaining deeply divisive and the 2020 election cycle creating constant cultural tension. Climate activism had gained significant youth momentum following Greta Thunberg's prominent appearance at the UN Climate Action Summit (September 23, 2019—the day after this sermon). In popular culture, television and streaming dominated (The Handmaid's Tale, Breaking Bad, Marvel content), and the NFL season had just begun, with the Denver Broncos drawing local sports attention. There was a sense of social fragmentation and competing urgent crises—political, environmental, and social—that characterized the American zeitgeist in late September 2019.
